chunk_summary: Early initiation of exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) within the first hour after birth, with on-demand feeding patterns, supports infant health, maternal recovery, and long-term benefits. EBF excludes all other foods or liquids for the first 6 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of initiating breastfeeding within the first hour after birth and maintaining skin-to-skin contact.
Learn the principles of exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) and its health benefits for both infant and mother.
Identify common cultural practices that disrupt EBF and strategies to address them.
Recognize proper breastfeeding techniques, including positioning and latch, to ensure effective feeding.
key_facts: Breastfeeding should be initiated within the first hour after birth to promote infant rooting and maternal oxytocin release.
Exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) means feeding only breast milk (no water, honey, or other liquids) for the first 6 months.
Newborns typically feed 8–12 times per 24 hours, with cluster feeding being common.
Breast milk is approximately 88% water and meets hydration needs, even in hot weather.
EBF reduces the risk of diarrheal and respiratory infections, supports neurodevelopment, and lowers maternal risks of breast and ovarian cancer.
Night feeds are important for maintaining milk supply due to prolactin peaks overnight.
reference_quote: ‘Breastfeeding should be initiated within the first hour of birth and exclusive breastfeeding should continue for the first six months of life.’ (World Health Organization, Reference 1)
